title: Collect and expose the minimum data needed
principle: Request, render, persist, log, and transmit only data required for the stated user task and retention period.
rationale: Every additional field increases breach impact, consent burden, error surface, and user hesitation.
applies_when: Designing forms, personalization, analytics, logs, profiles, exports, or administrative views.
exceptions: A legal or safety obligation may require specific data with documented purpose, access, and retention.
implementation: Map each datum to purpose, owner, access, retention, and deletion path.
Mask sensitive values and avoid placing secrets in URLs, DOM attributes, analytics, or client logs.
verification: Remove each field hypothetically and justify why the task would fail.
Inspect network, storage, logs, and rendered markup for unnecessary sensitive data.
accessibility: Privacy notices and controls are understandable and operable with assistive technology.
responsive: Narrow layouts do not reveal sensitive columns through overflow or accidental horizontal scroll.
performance: Minimized payloads reduce transfer, storage, and rendering cost.
browser_notes: Account for autofill, password managers, shared devices, and browser storage clearing.

title: Match destructive safeguards to consequence
principle: Use consequence-specific copy, permissions, review, confirmation, undo, and reauthentication in proportion to reversibility and blast radius.
rationale: Generic confirmations become habituated, while excessive friction on reversible actions trains users to dismiss warnings.
applies_when: An action deletes, publishes, pays, grants access, revokes access, or changes many records.
exceptions: A fully reversible low-impact action may rely on immediate undo instead of a modal confirmation.
implementation: State the object, scope, consequence, and reversibility.
Keep dangerous actions away from frequent safe actions and require explicit selection for bulk scope.
verification: Test accidental double activation, stale selection, permission changes, and retry.
Confirmation describes the actual current scope.
accessibility: Focus enters and returns correctly; warning text and action names are announced before commitment.
responsive: Destructive and cancel actions remain distinguishable when stacked and are not obscured by keyboards.
performance: Disable duplicate commits and make idempotency or conflict behavior explicit.
browser_notes: Do not rely on unload dialogs as the only safeguard.

title: Show useful errors without leaking sensitive detail
principle: Give the user actionable recovery information while withholding stack traces, secrets, internal topology, account enumeration, and unauthorized object details.
rationale: Opaque errors block recovery, but raw internal errors expose attack surface and private data.
applies_when: Authentication, authorization, validation, API, payment, upload, or server errors occur.
exceptions: Authorized developer tooling may show diagnostic detail in a controlled environment with redaction.
implementation: Map internal failures to stable user-safe messages and copyable incident IDs.
Log restricted diagnostics separately with redaction.
verification: Fuzz invalid and unauthorized inputs and inspect all response surfaces.
Recovery remains possible without exposing whether a protected resource exists.
accessibility: Safe messages remain specific enough to guide users and are associated or announced correctly.
responsive: Incident IDs wrap or copy without causing overflow.
performance: Error retry and logging paths are bounded and cannot amplify failures.
browser_notes: Production source maps and error overlays follow the deployment security policy.

title: Design for translation expansion and contraction
principle: Allow labels and content to expand, contract, wrap, and use different word boundaries without clipping or destroying hierarchy.
rationale: Fixed-width controls and English-sized placeholders fail when translations are longer, shorter, or structurally different.
applies_when: The product may be localized now or later.
exceptions: Strictly bounded machine identifiers may truncate with a full accessible and copyable value.
implementation: Use flexible containers, content-driven sizing, and representative pseudo-localization.
Keep copy outside image assets and component code.
verification: Run expansion, accented, no-space, and compact-string pseudo-locales.
Primary actions remain fully named and reachable.
accessibility: Truncated visible text still has a discoverable complete value and labels remain programmatically associated.
responsive: Combine pseudo-localization with minimum-width and large-text testing.
performance: Split locale resources without creating flashes of untranslated content.
browser_notes: Test wrapping, hyphenation, line breaking, and font fallback for target scripts.

title: Format data with locale-aware primitives
principle: Use locale- and timezone-aware formatters for dates, times, numbers, currency, lists, and plurals; store canonical values separately from display strings.
rationale: Hand-built formatting silently creates ambiguous dates, wrong currency precision, broken plural grammar, and daylight-saving errors.
applies_when: Rendering or accepting culturally formatted values.
exceptions: A protocol or machine-readable export may require a fixed standard format clearly labeled as such.
implementation: Use platform internationalization APIs or vetted wrappers.
Display timezone or currency context when ambiguity affects decisions.
verification: Test multiple locales, numbering systems, timezones, DST boundaries, and plural categories.
Round trips do not corrupt canonical values.
accessibility: Accessible names pronounce abbreviations and ambiguous numeric values clearly.
responsive: Formatted values can wrap or use documented compact representations without losing units.
performance: Reuse formatter instances and load only needed locale data.
browser_notes: Verify Intl feature and locale-data support against the baseline; polyfill selectively.

title: Use logical layout and isolate bidirectional content
principle: Express spatial relationships with logical properties, set language and direction correctly, and isolate user-supplied bidirectional fragments.
rationale: Left/right assumptions and unisolated mixed-direction values cause reversed layouts, misplaced affordances, and unreadable or misleading text.
applies_when: Supporting RTL languages or rendering mixed-script user content, identifiers, phone numbers, or addresses.
exceptions: Physical directions remain physical when the meaning is literally left or right, such as a map instruction.
implementation: Use inline/block logical properties and direction-aware icons.
Apply language metadata and bidirectional isolation to embedded values.
verification: Run a real RTL locale and mixed-direction fixtures.
Reading, focus, animation, and icon direction remain logical.
accessibility: Correct language and direction improve pronunciation and reading sequence.
responsive: Mirrored layouts retain action hierarchy and do not overflow.
performance: One logical stylesheet is preferable to duplicated directional bundles.
browser_notes: Verify logical property and bidi isolation support in the browser baseline.

title: Declare a browser baseline and enhance beyond it
principle: Publish the minimum supported browser capabilities from user evidence and business risk, then layer newer capabilities behind detection and usable fallbacks.
rationale: Undefined support produces accidental breakage, excess transpilation, and inconsistent decisions about modern APIs.
applies_when: Selecting platform features, build targets, polyfills, or test matrices.
exceptions: A controlled enterprise environment may use a narrower documented baseline.
implementation: Define essential task behavior at baseline and enhancements above it.
Use feature detection and CSS supports rather than user-agent branching.
verification: Primary tasks pass on every baseline target.
Unsupported enhancements degrade without missing content, focus, or actions.
accessibility: Assistive-technology/browser combinations are part of the baseline evidence.
responsive: Baseline testing covers input, zoom, and viewport variation.
performance: Avoid universal polyfills and legacy transforms beyond the policy.
browser_notes: Review the baseline on a scheduled cadence using analytics and known platform changes.

title: Test real platform behavior at risk boundaries
principle: Use automation for breadth and real browsers, devices, and assistive technologies for high-risk behavior that emulation cannot faithfully reproduce.
rationale: Input, viewport chrome, virtual keyboards, native controls, media, focus, and accessibility APIs differ in ways unit and DOM simulators do not model.
applies_when: Behavior depends on platform UI, device input, rendering engine, or accessibility integration.
exceptions: Pure deterministic data logic may remain covered by unit tests.
implementation: Identify risk boundaries and assign representative manual or device tests.
Record browser-specific workarounds with expiry conditions.
verification: Release evidence includes real-platform results for each high-risk boundary.
Known deviations have an owner and user impact assessment.
accessibility: Test keyboard, zoom, forced colors, screen readers, reduced motion, and voice where relevant.
responsive: Use real dynamic viewports, safe areas, orientation, and virtual keyboards for critical flows.
performance: Profile on a representative low-power device, not only desktop throttling.
browser_notes: Keep the matrix proportional to audience share, feature risk, and regression history.
